concept: Key guidelines: strided convolutions instead of pooling, Batch Normalization in both networks, specific activations (ReLU in generator, LeakyReLU in discriminator), and no fully-connected hidden layers.

options.text: They were perfectly stable and easy to train
They were highly unstable and frequently failed to train
They did not use neural networks at all

exercises.prompt: Why did researchers need to establish strict architectural guidelines like DCGAN?
exercises.explain: Before DCGANs, early GANs were notoriously unstable and hard to train, often producing chaotic noise instead of clear images.

cards.title: The Blueprint of GANs
cards.body: Ever tried building a house without a blueprint? It usually collapses! Early GANs were just like that—super unstable and prone to crashing. DCGAN introduced a strict architectural blueprint that finally made training stable and reliable.

A Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) is a set of architecture guidelines. Key rules include: replacing pooling layers with strided convolutions, using Batch Normalization in both networks, avoiding fully-connected hidden layers, using ReLU activation in the generator, and using LeakyReLU in the discriminator.
cards.title: The DCGAN Rules

cards.body: Imagine two artists collaborating. Instead of guessing how to paint, they use a checklist. The generator uses a 'detail-expanding' brush (ReLU & strided convolutions) and the discriminator uses a 'flaw-detecting' magnifying glass (LeakyReLU) to make sure they stay in perfect sync.
cards.title: The Collaborative Artists

cards.body: In a DCGAN generator, we feed in a vector of random numbers. The network uses fractional-strided convolutions to upsample this vector into a 64x64 pixel image of a cat, applying Batch Normalization at each step to prevent the colors and shapes from blowing up.
cards.title: Generating a Cat Image

cards.body: You might think that pooling layers (like MaxPool) are always good for downsampling images. But in DCGANs, spatial pooling is replaced with strided convolutions. This allows the network to learn its own downsampling and upsampling parameters!
cards.title: Why No Pooling Layers?
ReLU vs. LeakyReLU
cards.body: Let's compare the activation preferences of the Generator and the Discriminator in a standard DCGAN design.

left.text: Uses ReLU for all layers (except output which uses Tanh) to scale up features cleanly.

right.text: Uses LeakyReLU for all layers to allow a small gradient flow even when values are negative.

exercises.explain: DCGAN replaces traditional pooling layers (like MaxPool) with strided convolutions, allowing the network to learn its own spatial downsampling.
exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a key guideline of the DCGAN architecture?

options.text: It uses strided convolutions instead of traditional pooling layers.
It relies heavily on fully-connected hidden layers.
It uses sigmoid activations in all middle layers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: DCGAN guidelines recommend using Batch Normalization in both the generator and discriminator networks.
exercises.explain: True! Batch Normalization is applied in both the Generator and the Discriminator to stabilize the learning process.
The discriminator in a DCGAN uses LeakyReLU activation for its hidden layers to prevent dead neurons during downsampling.
exercises.prompt: While the generator uses ReLU in its hidden layers, the discriminator uses the ________ activation function.
exercises.sentence: While the generator uses ReLU in its hidden layers, the discriminator uses the LeakyReLU activation function.

exercises.prompt: Which system demonstrates a correct DCGAN discriminator design choice?

options.text: System A: Learns spatial downsampling via strided convolutions and uses LeakyReLU.
System B: Uses deterministic Max-Pooling and standard ReLU activations in the discriminator.

exercises.explain: System A is a classic DCGAN discriminator using LeakyReLU and strided convolutions, whereas System B's standard max-pooling can lose important spatial coordinate details needed for GAN stability.

options.text: Adding max-pooling layers and fully-connected hidden layers.
Replacing pooling with strided convolutions, using Batch Normalization, and removing fully-connected hidden layers.
Using LeakyReLU in the generator and pure ReLU in the discriminator.

exercises.prompt: Which set of features correctly describes the stabilizing architecture choices of a DCGAN?
exercises.explain: DCGAN guidelines recommend removing fully-connected hidden layers, using strided convolutions, Batch Normalization in both networks, ReLU in the generator, and LeakyReLU in the discriminator. In the next lesson, we will see how these rules are implemented in code!

title: DCGAN's Architecture Guidelines
